title: The inventory of sounds in dialects: on the problem of the origins of the Ukrainian language
author: Ishchenko, Oleksandr
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Introduction.  The article investigates the phonetic inventory of Ukrainian dialects as a quantitative parameter for addressing the problem of the origin of the Ukrainian language. The central concept is the inventory of sounds, defined as the full set of minimal phonetic units (vowels and consonants) in a language or dialect system. The study aims to determine whether differences in sound inventories across dialect groups can indicate the geographical origin of the Ukrainian language. 
  Methods.  The research applies a quantitative comparative method based on a hypothesis proposed by Quentin Atkinson (2011), which suggests a correlation between the size of a phoneme inventory and the age of a language. The author adapts this principle to Ukrainian dialects: the dialect area with the richest sound inventory is hypothesized to be the most probable origin zone. The analysis focuses on three major dialect groups: 
 
 Northern 
 South-western 
 South-eastern 
 
 Importantly, the study examines sounds rather than strictly phonemes, because sound inventories better reflect phonetic diversity. Data are compiled from linguistic atlases and descriptive studies. 
  Results.  
 
  Vowel inventories:  
 
 
 Northern dialect: 34 vowels 
 South-western dialect: 30 vowels 
 South-eastern dialect: 20 vowels 
 
 
  Consonant inventories:  
 
 
 Northern dialect: 54 consonants 
 South-western dialect: 66 consonants 
 South-eastern dialect: 53 consonants 
 
 
  Total sound inventories:  
 
 
 Northern: 88 sounds 
 South-western: 96 sounds 
 South-eastern: 73 sounds 
 
 Across all dialects: 
 
 Total: 119 sounds (50 vowels + 69 consonants) 
 
 The south-western dialect has the largest inventory, followed by the northern, and then the south-eastern dialect. 
  Discussion.  Applying the quantitative principle, the author interprets the results as evidence that: 
 
 The south-western dialect area is the most probable origin of the Ukrainian language. 
 The northern dialect is secondary in this respect. 
 The south-eastern dialect is the least likely origin zone. 
 
 The study proposes a three-stage model of language expansion: 
 
 Emergence in the south-western area 
 Spread to the northern territories 
 Later expansion into the south-eastern regions 
 
 This aligns with established views in dialectology that south-western and northern dialects are older, while south-eastern dialects are relatively recent. 
  Conclusion.  The article concludes that the size of the sound inventory is a meaningful quantitative indicator for linguistic origin. Based on this metric, the south-western dialect region is identified as the most likely cradle of the Ukrainian language. The study reinforces the value of quantitative phonetic analysis in historical linguistics, while acknowledging that such conclusions remain probabilistic rather than definitive.
